title: Generate requirements for a battery-powered handheld vacuum
stakeholder_needs:
Generate requirements for a battery-powered handheld vacuum
    cleaner for home use. It should run for at least 20 minutes,
    weigh under 1.5 kg, and meet EU safety standards.
analysis: Stakeholders: (1) End users/homeowners need convenient, lightweight, effective cleaning with predictable runtime and easy maintenance; (2) Retail/e-commerce/channel partners need clear specs, compliance marks, and low return rates; (3) Manufacturer/engineering need measurable performance targets, cost/complexity constraints, and testable requirements; (4) Regulatory/compliance for EU market requires CE marking and conformity to applicable directives/standards (e.g., LVD if applicable, EMC, RoHS, REACH, Battery Regulation, WEEE) and harmonized product safety standards for household appliances; (5) Service/support need replaceable consumables/filters, battery lifecycle expectations, and diagnostics; (6) Environmental stakeholders require battery safety, recyclability, and end-of-life handling. Derived requirements should cover: functional suction/cleaning, runtime >=20 min under defined mode, mass <1.5 kg including battery, battery/charger safety, noise/ergonomics, charging time, bin capacity, filtration, durability, packaging/labeling, and compliance documentation and testing. Key risk: ambiguous definitions for runtime and performance test conditions, and which EU standards apply (corded vs battery, charger type, intended surfaces).
clarification_questions: How should the 20-minute runtime be measured (power mode, battery age/state, with/without motorized brush, and test method/standard)?
What primary use cases/surfaces are in scope (quick spot-clean hard floors, carpets, pet hair, car interior), and do you require attachments (crevice tool, brush, motorized head)?
What charging approach is desired (USB-C vs dock/adapter, max charge time), and is the battery user-replaceable or sealed?

table.title: DFMEA - Battery-powered Handheld Vacuum Cleaner (Home Use, EU Market)
rows: Provide safe energy storage for >=20 min runtime without thermal event
Thermal runaway during charge or discharge
Fire/smoke, user injury, property damage, recall, regulatory non-compliance
Internal cell short, overcharge, mechanical damage, inadequate BMS limits, poor cell matching
Cell supplier qualification, BMS OV/UV/OCP design, cell spacing/insulation, pack mechanical protection, derating analysis
Pack abuse testing (UN 38.3, IEC 62133-2), BMS fault injection tests, end-of-line (EOL) pack functional test
Add redundant temperature sensing + independent hardware cutoff; increase separator/insulation; tighten cell grading; add CID/NTC verification at EOL
BMS architecture review scheduled; supplier PPAP requested; thermal model updated
Define safe-operating-area and pack fault handling; reuse BMS cutoff thresholds across platforms
Cell Supplier A / BMS PCB Supplier
Ensure CE file includes battery safety evidence and traceability
Provide stable output voltage/current to motor across SOC and temperature
Insufficient runtime (<20 min) under defined mode
Customer dissatisfaction, returns, negative reviews, fails spec
Cell capacity fade, high internal resistance, BMS early cutoff, high motor load, poor thermal management
Energy budget sizing, motor efficiency target, BMS cutoff calibration, low-IR cells, thermal path design
Runtime validation test at 23°C on standard dust load; production capacity sampling; BMS log review
Define runtime test conditions in requirement; increase pack capacity margin; optimize motor control for efficiency; improve airflow cooling around pack
Draft runtime test method created; energy model indicates +10% Wh needed
Always tie runtime claim to mode, nozzle, dust load, temp; reuse energy model template
Marketing claim must match verified test configuration
Charge battery safely and within specified time
Battery damage, thermal event, non-compliance (LVD/EMC), user hazard
Wrong adapter supplied, PSU component drift, counterfeit charger, poor regulation under load
Use certified PSU, keyed connector, input/output OVP, supplier audit, labeling
Incoming inspection, PSU functional test, OVP verification, EMC pre-scan
Implement charger authentication or unique connector; add TVS and secondary OVP in product; tighten packaging controls to prevent mix-up
Connector keying concept selected; PSU vendor provides CB report
Control of accessories is critical; reuse keyed interface across SKUs
Ensure DoC lists correct PSU models and ratings
Generate airflow and suction to meet cleaning performance
Loss of suction, overheating, potential smoke, product failure
Insulation breakdown, bearing failure leading to rotor rub, manufacturing defect, moisture ingress
Motor derating, varnish spec, bearing selection, conformal coating where needed, incoming motor QA
EOL spin test, current draw monitoring, thermal rise test, HALT screening sample
Add thermal fuse or electronic motor temp limit; increase insulation class; add moisture protection to airflow path; tighten motor supplier process controls
Thermal limit algorithm drafted; supplier requested for insulation class upgrade proposal
Monitor current draw signature for early failure; reuse motor screening thresholds
Balance efficiency vs noise and heat
Provide effective debris pickup on typical home surfaces
Airflow restriction or clogging in nozzle/duct
Reduced cleaning, higher current draw, shorter runtime, user frustration
Geometry traps hair/debris, sharp turns, insufficient cross-section, poor debris separation
CFD design, clog risk FMEA, hair pickup features, duct smoothness spec
User clog test, airflow measurement, pressure drop test, field feedback monitoring
Increase minimum duct diameter; add anti-tangle brush/nozzle comb; add clog indicator via pressure sensor estimate; improve bin cyclone separation
CFD iteration planned; prototype nozzle comb designed
Define clogging test with hair mix; reuse pressure-drop limits in future designs
Ensure easy user-access for clearing clogs
Capture fine dust and protect motor while maintaining airflow
Dust leakage to user environment, reduced filtration, motor wear, complaints
Poor gasket design, user misassembly after cleaning, tolerance stack-up, latch weakness
Poka-yoke filter geometry, robust latch, gasket compression spec, user instructions
Visual inspection, dust leak test sample, fit check, user trial validation
Add keyed filter frame + interlock preventing operation if filter not seated; improve gasket material; add clear UI prompt for filter install
Keyed feature in CAD; interlock concept under review
Design for correct assembly by novice users; reuse interlock approach for consumables
Maintain filtration efficiency over life with acceptable airflow
Filter clogging too quickly / high pressure drop
Suction loss, runtime loss, overheating risk, frequent maintenance
Media too dense, insufficient area, high dust loading, lack of pre-separation, humid dust cakes
Media selection trials, cyclone/pre-filter design, service interval spec
Airflow vs dust loading test, thermal rise test, user maintenance study
Increase filter area or pleat count; add washable pre-filter; add maintenance indicator based on runtime/pressure estimate; update manual cleaning interval
Dust loading test matrix defined; candidate media samples ordered
Specify dust type and loading rate in tests; reuse dust-loading protocol
Consider allergy claims only if validated
Withstand drops and daily handling while keeping mass <1.5 kg
Cracking after drop leading to sharp edges or internal damage
User injury, battery/motor damage, increased returns
Brittle resin, thin walls, stress concentrators, poor screw boss design, low-temp impact
Material spec with impact modifiers, ribbing, boss design guidelines, drop analysis
Drop test to IEC/EN household appliance expectations, incoming resin certs, visual inspection
Add radii and ribs at high-stress zones; change resin grade; add energy-absorbing features; validate at low temperature
FEA hotspots identified; resin alternatives shortlisted
Early FEA + drop prototypes prevent late redesign; reuse boss design rules
Ensure no access to live parts post-drop
Maintain airtight suction path and prevent dust leakage
Seal compression set or leak over time
Reduced suction, dust leakage, noise change, customer complaints
Wrong elastomer, heat aging, chemical exposure (cleaners), poor groove design, assembly damage
Material selection (EPDM/silicone), compression design, assembly work instructions, lubricant control
Leak test sample, suction performance test, accelerated aging test
Add leak check at EOL for every unit; redesign gasket groove for controlled compression; specify aging resistance; add visual poka-yoke for gasket presence
EOL leak test concept (pressure decay) quoted; gasket groove revision in progress
EOL leak test is effective for multiple leak paths; reuse pressure-decay method
Track gasket lot for field issue containment
Allow user to control power/modes and understand status
Power switch sticks ON or OFF
Unintended operation, battery drain, overheating risk, poor usability
Contamination ingress, poor switch spec, mechanical tolerance, wear, inadequate sealing
Switch selection to rated cycles, debounce/control logic, ingress protection features, design clearance
EOL functional test, life-cycle button test, contamination test sampling
Add software auto-off timeout; improve switch sealing; add mode confirmation via LED; increase cycle-life requirement in spec
Auto-off feature implemented in beta firmware; switch vendor cycle data reviewed
Combine mechanical + firmware mitigations for UI reliability; reuse auto-off across products
Auto-off must not violate user expectations
Meet EU EMC and operate reliably without resets
EMC susceptibility causes reset or uncontrolled behavior
Unexpected shutoff, mode change, user dissatisfaction, possible safety concern
Poor grounding, inadequate filtering, antenna effects, ESD to UI, motor noise coupling
EMC design rules, filters, shielding, PCB layout review, separation of power/signal
EMC pre-compliance tests (EN 55014-1/-2), ESD test (IEC 61000-4-2), conducted/radiated tests
Add common-mode choke and RC snubbers; improve ground return; add watchdog and safe-state handling; increase ESD protection on UI lines
Pre-scan booked; layout changes proposed for rev B PCB
Do pre-scan early with motor running; reuse proven filter network
Document EMC evidence in technical file
Provide reliable charging connection over product life
Intermittent charging due to contact wear/contamination
No charge, reduced availability, returns, support burden
Oxidation, misalignment, weak spring force, debris, plating too thin
Gold plating spec, contact force design, alignment features, dust covers
Charge detection logic, EOL charge test, salt fog test sample, user trials
Increase plating thickness; add self-wiping contact geometry; add UI error indication for poor contact; add dock alignment chamfers/magnets
Contact vendor engaged; alignment feature added to dock CAD
Provide clear user feedback for charging faults; reuse contact spec template
Consider USB-C only if full compliance and robustness verified
Prevent over-discharge and ensure safe shutdown
Over-discharge leading to cell damage or swelling
Reduced battery life, potential venting, warranty cost
Incorrect UV cutoff, sleep current too high, firmware bug, cell imbalance
BMS requirements review, UV threshold validation, low-Iq design, balancing algorithm
BMS firmware unit tests, long-term storage test, pack impedance/capacity trending
Add independent UV comparator; reduce quiescent draw; add storage mode; expand firmware regression tests covering corner SOC/temperature
Low-Iq component swap identified; additional test cases drafted
Validate storage behavior for retail shelf life; reuse UV thresholds and tests
Keep surfaces and internals within safe touch and component limits
Surface temperature exceeds limit during continuous use
User discomfort/burn risk, non-compliance with safety standards, product damage
Insufficient airflow cooling, blocked exhaust, high motor load, sensor misplacement, poor thermal path
Thermal design review, sensor placement spec, derating curves, exhaust grill design
Thermal testing per applicable EN/IEC appliance standard, worst-case clog simulation, calibration checks
Add temperature-based power derate; relocate/add NTC near hotspot; redesign exhaust to reduce blockage; add warning in IFU for blocked vents
Hotspot mapping planned; derate curve proposal drafted
Test with clogged filter/nozzle scenarios; reuse derating strategy
Ensure touch temperature limits align with selected harmonized standard

messages.content:
Generate requirements for a battery-powered handheld vacuum
    cleaner for home use. It should run for at least 20 minutes,
    weigh under 1.5 kg, and meet EU safety standards.

Stakeholders: (1) End users/homeowners need convenient, lightweight, effective cleaning with predictable runtime and easy maintenance; (2) Retail/e-commerce/channel partners need clear specs, compliance marks, and low return rates; (3) Manufacturer/engineering need measurable performance targets, cost/complexity constraints, and testable requirements; (4) Regulatory/compliance for EU market requires CE marking and conformity to applicable directives/standards (e.g., LVD if applicable, EMC, RoHS, REACH, Battery Regulation, WEEE) and harmonized product safety standards for household appliances; (5) Service/support need replaceable consumables/filters, battery lifecycle expectations, and diagnostics; (6) Environmental stakeholders require battery safety, recyclability, and end-of-life handling. Derived requirements should cover: functional suction/cleaning, runtime >=20 min under defined mode, mass <1.5 kg including battery, battery/charger safety, noise/ergonomics, charging time, bin capacity, filtration, durability, packaging/labeling, and compliance documentation and testing. Key risk: ambiguous definitions for runtime and performance test conditions, and which EU standards apply (corded vs battery, charger type, intended surfaces).
